title: In The Name Of The Grandfather

jokes.text: A home and garden show? Mom, you said we were going to a video game expo! / You told me we were gonna pick up trash by the freeway!
jokes.setup: Kids complaining about being misled about their destination
jokes.punchline: Lisa's complaint escalates the absurdity - she was told they'd pick up trash, making her equally disappointed
jokes.explanation: Solid escalation joke where Lisa's complaint is even more ridiculous than Bart's, showing how Marge deceived each kid differently

jokes.text: You told me something, but I wasn't listening!
jokes.setup: Homer joining the pattern of complaints about being misled
jokes.punchline: Homer admits he wasn't listening, making his complaint meaningless but perfectly in character
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - he wants to complain but admits he wasn't paying attention. Maximum economy and character truth.

jokes.text: There's a day we'll never get back. / I was so bored.
jokes.setup: Kids practicing singing exercises
jokes.punchline: Their deadpan reactions to what was presumably terrible singing practice
jokes.explanation: Functional reaction humor but fairly predictable - kids being bored at family activity

jokes.text: Look! A welcome mat that tells you you're weight.
jokes.setup: Homer excited about home and garden products
jokes.punchline: A welcome mat that measures weight - absurd product concept
jokes.explanation: Decent absurd product gag that fits the home show setting

jokes.text: Your weight is... Warranty voided.
jokes.setup: Expectation that the mat will reveal someone's weight
jokes.punchline: Instead it just says warranty voided, suggesting someone broke it by being too heavy
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion - instead of revealing weight, the mat essentially calls someone too fat. Good economy and surprise.

jokes.text: The Sprinkle King, Sprinkle Queen, and Deluxe Sprinkle King are the Simon and Garfunkel plus one of hose nozzle dispersal arrays.
jokes.setup: Homer getting excited about sprinkler products
jokes.punchline: Comparing sprinklers to Simon and Garfunkel 'plus one' - treating lawn equipment like a musical act
jokes.explanation: Homer's characteristic over-enthusiasm with an odd comparison. A bit wordy but captures his voice.

jokes.text: What is that soothing sound? Maybe it's beef stew!
jokes.setup: Homer hearing the sound of a hot tub
jokes.punchline: His first guess for a soothing sound is beef stew
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - connecting a relaxing sound to food. Excellent character integration and surprising connection.

jokes.text: Getting a back rub from an orgasm.
jokes.setup: Someone asking what a hot tub feels like
jokes.punchline: Homer's inappropriately sexual and grammatically confused description
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer inappropriate comment with confused grammar ('from an orgasm'). Moderately funny but relies on shock value.

jokes.text: I'll be less stressed! And I'm really friggin' stressed!
jokes.setup: Bart making his case for why they should buy the hot tub
jokes.punchline: Claiming he's really stressed as a 10-year-old kid
jokes.explanation: Funny incongruity of a child claiming to be stressed. Good character moment for Bart's manipulative side.

jokes.text: Now I know why lobsters let out that joyful squeal when they're dropped in the pot.
jokes.setup: Homer enjoying the hot tub
jokes.punchline: Comparing his pleasure to lobsters being boiled alive, calling their death screams 'joyful'
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with Homer's characteristic obliviousness. Brilliant absurd comparison that reframes lobster death screams as joy.

jokes.text: We're starting to attract swingers.
jokes.setup: The family enjoying their hot tub
jokes.punchline: Marge noticing they're attracting the wrong kind of attention
jokes.explanation: Good situational comedy with Marge as the voice of reason noticing the awkward implications.

jokes.text: Now with an opposing point of view, bubbles!
jokes.setup: Discussion about whether to get out of the hot tub
jokes.punchline: Homer treating the hot tub bubbles as having a valid opposing viewpoint in the debate
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment where Homer anthropomorphizes bubbles as debate participants. Very creative and in character.

jokes.text: Afraid you'll get your mustache wet? Well, yes.
jokes.setup: Homer taunting Flanders about joining them in the hot tub
jokes.punchline: Flanders' literal, honest response to what was meant as mockery
jokes.explanation: Perfect Flanders response - taking Homer's sarcastic question literally and honestly. Great character interplay.

jokes.text: Funny word, urgent. Urgent. Urgent.
jokes.setup: Remembering there was an urgent note on the fridge
jokes.punchline: Homer getting distracted by the word 'urgent' itself instead of the urgency
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer ADD moment - getting distracted by the sound of a word rather than its meaning. Very in character.

jokes.text: Probably a reminder to relax more.
jokes.setup: Trying to rationalize not checking the urgent note
jokes.punchline: Homer assuming an urgent note would tell him to relax, perfect circular logic
jokes.explanation: Homer's perfect rationalization - an urgent note must be telling him to do what he's already doing. Great self-serving logic.

jokes.text: They're blanks, Abe.
jokes.setup: What appears to be a firing squad situation with Grandpa
jokes.punchline: Revealing the guns have blanks, subverting the dark expectation
jokes.explanation: Dark humor subversion revealing it's not actually an execution. Functional but not particularly innovative.

jokes.text: While you were lying here soaking like a bunch of dirty dishes, I was lonelier than Estes Kefauver at a meeting of Murder Incorporated!
jokes.setup: Grandpa angry at the family for missing his event
jokes.punchline: Extremely obscure historical reference comparing his loneliness to a crime fighter at a mob meeting
jokes.explanation: Incredibly obscure but brilliant reference that works perfectly for Grandpa's character. High originality but may go over many heads.

jokes.text: That actually makes sense. Look it up!
jokes.setup: Following up the obscure Estes Kefauver reference
jokes.punchline: Grandpa defending his obscure reference and challenging people to verify it
jokes.explanation: Perfect follow-up that acknowledges the obscurity while insisting on its accuracy. Meta-humor about the reference itself.

jokes.text: Oh, Dad! I never knew you had dreams or emotions.
jokes.setup: Grandpa expressing regret about missed opportunities
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact surprise that his father has feelings
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's emotional obtuseness toward his father. Dark but relatable family comedy.

jokes.text: Who said anything about a jar?
jokes.setup: Grandpa saying he wants to do things before becoming 'dust in a jar'
jokes.punchline: Homer implying Grandpa won't even get the dignity of a jar for his ashes
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with perfect economy. Homer's casual cruelty about his father's future cremation is shocking and funny.

jokes.text: Pitch in a negro league. I can at least think of at least two problems with that.
jokes.setup: Reading Grandpa's bucket list item about playing in the Negro League
jokes.punchline: Homer noting the obvious problems (racial and temporal) with this goal
jokes.explanation: Functional comedy about the obvious impossibility of the request. Homer showing rare logical thinking.

jokes.text: Welcome to Ireland. Also known as the Emerald Isle, Potatoville, East Boston, Freckled Bog, the Land of Poetry, and the Land of Bad Poetry.
jokes.setup: Tourist guide listing Ireland's nicknames
jokes.punchline: Mix of legitimate and insulting names, ending with 'Land of Bad Poetry' after 'Land of Poetry'
jokes.explanation: Good escalating list with mix of real and fake nicknames. The poetry contradiction is a nice touch.

jokes.text: On your left, you'll see Western Ireland. On your right, you'll see Kathy Ireland. Hi, everyone!
jokes.setup: Standard tour guide patter about seeing Western Ireland
jokes.punchline: Unexpected appearance of model/actress Kathy Ireland as a tourist attraction
jokes.explanation: Simple name pun with celebrity cameo. Functional but relies on random celebrity appearance.

jokes.text: Dunkilderry is so quaint. It looks like the puzzle at the pediatrician's office.
jokes.setup: Marge commenting on the quaint Irish village
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to those generic pastoral puzzles in waiting rooms
jokes.explanation: Excellent observational humor that perfectly captures the generic 'quaint village' aesthetic. Very relatable reference.

jokes.text: In your face, Belgium!
jokes.setup: Learning Ireland is at the forefront of Europe's tech boom
jokes.punchline: Bart randomly taunting Belgium for no apparent reason
jokes.explanation: Random, absurd nationalism from Bart. The specificity of Belgium makes it funnier than a generic country.

jokes.text: Bart, if you hate Belgium so much, maybe I should take your Tintins away!
jokes.setup: Bart's anti-Belgium sentiment
jokes.punchline: Marge threatens to take away his Belgian comic books, exposing the irony
jokes.explanation: Smart follow-up that exposes Bart's hypocrisy through Tintin reference. Shows good knowledge of Belgian culture.

jokes.text: No, I'll be good!
jokes.setup: Marge's threat about taking away Tintin comics
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately backing down when his entertainment is threatened
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Bart's priorities. Simple but effective conclusion to the Belgium sequence.

jokes.text: Run! Run you big sack of glue!
jokes.setup: Old Irish pub owner watching horse racing
jokes.punchline: Calling a racehorse a 'big sack of glue' - referencing what happens to old horses
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with specific horse-racing knowledge. Good character establishment for the bitter Irish pub owner.

jokes.text: It's been years since I've sold so much as a 'pint.'
jokes.setup: Tom lamenting his lack of business
jokes.punchline: Mispronouncing 'pint' after being away from the business so long
jokes.explanation: Clever detail showing how out of practice Tom is. Good character-building through language.

jokes.text: Don't you mean pint? / It's been so long, I've forgotten how to say it!
jokes.setup: Homer correcting Tom's pronunciation
jokes.punchline: Tom admitting he's forgotten basic pub terminology from disuse
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the language joke, showing just how far Tom has fallen from his pub-keeping roots.

jokes.text: It's a joke to even call this place a 'pube.'
jokes.setup: Tom's continued language degradation
jokes.punchline: Now mispronouncing 'pub' as 'pube' - creating an unfortunate alternate meaning
jokes.explanation: Continues the language degradation theme but relies on accidental profanity. Less clever than the pint joke.

jokes.text: Such a beautiful language.
jokes.setup: German Krusty speaking on TV in German
jokes.punchline: Lisa's sarcastic appreciation of German as 'beautiful' when it's known for being harsh
jokes.explanation: Simple irony about German's harsh sound. Functional but predictable language stereotype humor.

jokes.text: Here's some Bushmills whiskey, served in a potato, floating in a Guinness stout.
jokes.setup: Homer demanding an Irish drink
jokes.punchline: Extremely over-the-top Irish stereotype combination
jokes.explanation: Good absurd escalation of Irish stereotypes. Over-the-top but self-aware about the stereotyping.

jokes.text: Who would like to know our secret recipe? I'm not going to tell you. Sure, if I did, it wouldn't be a secret.
jokes.setup: Tour guide asking who wants to know the secret recipe
jokes.punchline: Immediately refusing and explaining the obvious logic
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke about the obvious nature of secrets. Solid tour guide humor.

jokes.text: Well. You may know the ingredients, but you don't know the ratio. / 50-50?
jokes.setup: Tour guide trying to maintain some mystery about the recipe
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately guessing the most obvious ratio for a two-ingredient mixture
jokes.explanation: Homer's simpleton logic deflating pretension. Good character moment with efficient setup/payoff.

jokes.text: From now on, you're Francis *. / Bill Jones. / Seamus Fitzpatrick. / You're Liam Fitzpatrick, and you're brothers with him. / I said you're brothers! I didn't say you got along!
jokes.setup: Immigration officer giving people Irish names
jokes.punchline: Creating instant family relationships and then explaining they don't have to like each other
jokes.explanation: Absurd immigration humor with good escalation to the brotherly conflict. Creative concept.

jokes.text: But I'm not sure I like the idea of getting a gift in return for kissing. What kind of a message is that for Lisa and Maggie?
jokes.setup: Marge considering kissing the Blarney Stone for the gift of gab
jokes.punchline: Worrying about teaching her daughters to kiss for rewards
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge overthinking a tourist tradition. Great character integration with her protective parenting.

jokes.text: Except for Stone Phillips. Do you have one of him here? If so, he's our your him.
jokes.setup: Marge saying America doesn't have stones worth kissing
jokes.punchline: Mentioning news anchor Stone Phillips and getting confused about pronouns
jokes.explanation: Weak pun on Stone Phillips' name with confusing delivery. The pronoun confusion doesn't add much.

jokes.text: K-I-S-S M-Y A-S-S spells kiss my ass.
jokes.setup: Presumably Bart carving something inappropriate in the Blarney Stone
jokes.punchline: Spelling out a profane message letter by letter
jokes.explanation: Basic Bart misbehavior. In character but relies on simple profanity for shock value.

jokes.text: I just had a nightmare! That I was back with your mother! Oh, how I miss her.
jokes.setup: Grandpa waking up from what he calls a nightmare
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting himself by saying he misses his wife
jokes.explanation: Perfect contradiction showing Grandpa's complex feelings about his late wife. Great emotional confusion.

jokes.text: I have to say, for blind drunks, you had a excellent knowledge of Irish real estate law.
jokes.setup: Explaining how Homer and Grandpa bought the pub while drunk
jokes.punchline: Complimenting their inexplicable legal expertise while intoxicated
jokes.explanation: Absurd detail that makes the drunk purchase more ridiculous. Good specificity about real estate knowledge.

jokes.text: This brandy is fifty years old! Better toss it.
jokes.setup: Homer examining old alcohol in the pub
jokes.punchline: Misunderstanding that aged alcohol is valuable, thinking it's spoiled
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer ignorance about something he should know as a drinker. Great economy and character consistency.

jokes.text: I'm in AA. / Who are you? Stuttering Fonzie?
jokes.setup: Irish man declining a drink by saying he's in AA
jokes.punchline: Homer's confusion about the initials, referencing Fonzie's 'Ayyyy'
jokes.explanation: Decent misunderstanding joke with pop culture reference. Homer's confusion is in character.

jokes.text: Our first sale. / Our first refund.
jokes.setup: Homer celebrating their first customer interaction
jokes.punchline: Grandpa immediately noting it was a refund, not a sale
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Homer's optimism with Grandpa's realism. Efficient back-and-forth.

jokes.text: I'm late for a teleconference in Hong Kong, you fat gob!
jokes.setup: Modern Irish businessman refusing drink
jokes.punchline: Professional excuse followed by traditional Irish insult
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between modern Ireland and traditional insults. Character represents cultural change.

jokes.text: Ireland doesn't like pubs anymore. It's as if Danish people stopped liking sleek modern design. / Bite your tongue! That will never ever happen!
jokes.setup: Homer comparing Ireland losing interest in pubs to another cultural impossibility
jokes.punchline: Lisa's passionate defense of Danish design sensibilities
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd comparison with Lisa's unexpected expertise in Danish design. Very specific cultural knowledge.

jokes.text: You wanna slow dance with a goat, I'll be doing a lot of things, but judging ain't one of them.
jokes.setup: Moe explaining his permissive bar policy
jokes.punchline: Extremely specific and disturbing example of what he won't judge
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe depravity with his characteristic non-judgmental attitude. Disturbing but funny escalation.

jokes.text: The law? That jerk!
jokes.setup: Discussion about breaking the law
jokes.punchline: Moe treating 'the law' as a personal enemy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe attitude with maximum economy. Treating abstract concepts as personal enemies is very in character.

jokes.text: All I remember about him is he was part of a complete breakfast.
jokes.setup: Moe looking for his grandfather based on a childhood photo
jokes.punchline: His only memory is a cereal box reference
jokes.explanation: Absurd family memory that suggests his grandfather was a cereal mascot. Good Moe randomness.

jokes.text: Well, Da. It looks like all your 'dur' have come 'ter.'
jokes.setup: Homer trying to speak with an Irish accent
jokes.punchline: Mangling 'dreams have come true' with fake Irish pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Homer's terrible attempt at Irish accent. In character but fairly predictable tourist behavior.

jokes.text: Look at all this Euro dough. These shamrock sure love their tobaccy.
jokes.setup: Homer looking at their earnings from the smokeasy
jokes.punchline: Using 'shamrock' as slang for Irish people and 'tobaccy' for tobacco
jokes.explanation: Homer's continued terrible Irish stereotyping. Somewhat forced wordplay with shamrock.

jokes.text: Throw them in the potty wagon. I mean the us wagon.
jokes.setup: Irish police officer giving orders
jokes.punchline: Accidentally saying 'potty wagon' and correcting to 'us wagon' (paddy wagon)
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay on paddy wagon with self-correction. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: America is the New York Yankees of countries. Powerful and respected until the year 2000.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to defend America to get out of jail
jokes.punchline: Comparing America to the Yankees but noting both declined after 2000
jokes.explanation: Clever comparison with unexpected twist that undermines America. Good structure with specific date reference.

jokes.text: Knute Rockne, Larry Bird and especially Mr. Potato Head.
jokes.setup: Homer listing famous Irish people he admires
jokes.punchline: Including a children's toy in his list of Irish heroes
jokes.explanation: Good escalation to absurdity with Mr. Potato Head. Homer's characteristic cultural confusion.

jokes.text: So I hope you'll forgive two well-meaning Americans for trying to take Ireland back to the good old days of Angela's Ashes.
jokes.setup: Homer making his case for leniency
jokes.punchline: Referencing a book about Irish poverty and misery as 'the good old days'
jokes.explanation: Dark irony referencing a memoir of Irish poverty as nostalgic. Shows Homer's cultural ignorance cleverly.

jokes.text: It got a lot nicer since we sent all our incompetent half-wits to America. Where you, for some reason, made them police officers.
jokes.setup: Judge commenting on Ireland's improvement
jokes.punchline: Explaining they exported their problems to America, who made them cops
jokes.explanation: Sharp commentary on Irish-American police stereotypes. Good reversal where Ireland insults America.

jokes.text: Oh, Jesus. / Better put a little ouchy spray on it.
jokes.setup: Wiggum presumably hurting himself upon arrival
jokes.punchline: Childish response to injury
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum incompetence. 'Ouchy spray' is perfectly childish for his character.

jokes.text: Boy! I better call for some backup. This is unit 19--
jokes.setup: Wiggum trying to radio for backup
jokes.punchline: Gets cut off mid-transmission, implying he's been incapacitated
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum incompetence with physical comedy ending. Predictable but in character.
